hero.tagline: Yoga for all group, all profession and all religions
hero.subtagline: No special equipment. No prior experience. Just the willingness to begin. Classes for all ages, all levels, all backgrounds.

about.schoolDesc1: Nibedita Yoga Training Centre was founded with a single conviction: that yoga belongs to everyone — every profession, every age, every background. You need no special clothing, no equipment, and no prior experience. Just the willingness to begin.
about.schoolDesc2: Our practice works on every dimension of health simultaneously. The asanas strengthen muscles and joints, tone the spine, and keep the skeletal system supple — while working on the internal organs, glands, and nervous system to keep every system of the body functioning at its full strength.
about.founderQuote: A complete science of life that originated in India many thousands of years ago. It is the oldest system of personal development in the world.
about.fourSystemsDesc: Our curriculum is designed to keep all four primary systems of the body fit, healthy, and functioning at full capacity.
about.teacherBio1: Nibedita Yoga Training Centre was established to develop three dimensions of health — mental, physical, and social well-being — while keeping the body's four main systems — circulatory, respiratory, excretory, and digestive — fit, healthy, and strong.
about.teacherBio2: Whether you come seeking fitness, relief from a specific condition, or simply a steadier life — these goals are all achievable through sincere, regular practice.
about.teacherBio3: Classes are structured to suit all ages and all levels. Absolute beginners are as welcome as long-time practitioners.
about.accessSpaceDesc: A 6×4 ft mat is all you need

yoga.heading: The History & Science of Yoga
yoga.subheading: Deepen your understanding — from ancient philosophy to modern neuroscience.

courses.heading: Courses & Self-Help Library
courses.subheading: Eight therapeutic programmes. Fifty asanas. Guided by the oldest system of personal development the world has ever known.
courses.heroSubheading: Eight therapeutic programmes built on three pillars of yoga practice — Asana, Pranayama, and Kriya.
courses.programmesSubheading: Each programme is curated for a specific health goal. Your instructor will recommend the most suitable one after your trial class.
courses.programmeNote: All programmes are delivered in group batch format. Individual timings and batch schedules are shown in your Members Portal after enrollment.
courses.navalDesc1: The navel (nabhi) is considered the body's centre of gravity in classical yoga. When displaced — through strain, poor posture, or sudden movement — it can trigger a cascade of seemingly unrelated symptoms throughout the body.
courses.navalDesc2: Our Naval Correction Programme uses a precise sequence of asanas and kriyas to detect displacement and restore the navel to its natural position — typically in one or two sessions.
courses.navalQ1: How do I know if my navel is displaced?
courses.navalA1: Symptoms vary widely: persistent loose motions or constipation, abdominal cramping, back pain, energy drain, or a "pulling" sensation near the navel. Your instructor will check.
courses.navalA2: Most cases correct in 1–2 focused sessions. Mild cases often resolve in a single class.
courses.navalA3: No — the sequence involves gentle asanas and breath-work. There is no manipulation or physical pressure.
courses.navalBadgeDesc: Often recommended as the first programme — a displaced navel can mask many other conditions.
courses.curriculumSubheading: Every programme is built on the same three-pillar foundation — calibrated for your specific health goal.
courses.pillar1Desc: Ten systematic breathing exercises spanning Sahaj Pranayam (1–3, 6), Anulom Vilom, Sitali, Suriyaved, Bhraman Pranayam, Kapal Bhati, and Vastrika.
courses.pillar2Desc: A structured warm-up sequence covering neck, shoulder, wrist, ankle, and hip mobilisation — preparing the body for deeper asana practice.
courses.pillar3Desc: 50 named asanas organised across Beginner, Intermediate, and Advanced stages. Each student progresses through stages as recommended by their instructor.
courses.pranayamaSubheading: Taught in sequence across the programme. Each targets specific physiological systems.
courses.kriyaHeading: Kriya Practice — The Hidden Third Pillar
courses.kriyaDesc: Beyond asana and pranayama, Kriyas — cleansing and purification techniques — form an essential part of advanced practice. These are taught individually and not listed in the public library. Enquire with your instructor.
courses.libraryDesc: Browse all 91 practices in our curriculum — with step-by-step instructions, contraindications, and health benefits for each.
courses.libraryBullet1Desc: Filter by type (Pranayama, Exercise, Asana, Kriya) or by health goal.
courses.libraryBullet2Desc: Every practice includes detailed how-to steps and safety notes.
courses.libraryBullet3Desc: Know when not to practise — important for therapeutic programmes.
courses.libraryBullet4Desc: See which programmes each practice belongs to.

programmes.navalSubtitle: Restore your body's centre of gravity

wellness.heading: Wellness Guide
wellness.subheading: Daily food, water, and practice guidelines from our curriculum.
wellness.sivaQuote: Yoga is not about touching your toes. It is what you learn on the way down.
wellness.waterSubheading: 7 glasses across the day — timing matters
wellness.foodTimingSubheading: Optimal meal windows for digestive health
wellness.practiceRulesSubheading: 15 guidelines from the Nibedita Yoga curriculum
wellness.practiceRule1: Practise on an empty stomach — at least 3–4 hours after a full meal.
wellness.practiceRule2: Morning practice is preferred; evening is equally valid.
wellness.practiceRule3: Wear loose, comfortable clothing — no tight waistbands.
wellness.practiceRule4: Use a clean mat or blanket. Keep the space clear and well-ventilated.
wellness.practiceRule5: Empty the bowels before practice if possible.
wellness.practiceRule6: Do not strain or force any posture. Work within your comfortable range.
wellness.practiceRule7: Breathe normally unless a specific breathing instruction is given.
wellness.practiceRule8: Do not hold your breath inadvertently during asanas.
wellness.practiceRule9: Rest in Shavasana for at least 5 minutes after completing the sequence.
wellness.practiceRule10: Women should avoid inverted postures during menstruation.
wellness.practiceRule11: Inform your instructor of any recent injury, surgery, or new medication.
wellness.practiceRule12: Do not practise in direct sunlight or in a very cold environment.
wellness.practiceRule13: Keep a gap of at least 30 minutes between bath and practice.
wellness.practiceRule14: Consistency matters more than duration — 30 minutes daily is better than 3 hours once a week.
wellness.practiceRule15: Observe your body's response. Stop and rest if you feel dizzy, nauseous, or any sharp pain.
wellness.dietSubheading: Classical yoga categorises food into three gunas — qualities that affect mind and body.
wellness.dietSattvicDesc: Fresh, natural, minimally processed foods that promote clarity, calm, and health. Ideal for practitioners.
wellness.dietSattvicExamples: Fresh fruits, vegetables, whole grains, legumes, milk, ghee, honey, nuts
wellness.dietRajasicDesc: Foods that stimulate the mind and body. Moderate amounts are acceptable; excess disturbs mental peace.
wellness.dietRajasicExamples: Spicy food, onion, garlic, coffee, tea, pickles, excess salt
wellness.dietTamasicDesc: Foods that create heaviness, lethargy, and dullness. Best minimised or avoided during active practice.
wellness.dietTamasicExamples: Stale food, meat, alcohol, excessive sugar, fried foods, processed food
